title: Standard Deviation & Variance 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Free standard deviation and variance calculator for samples and populations. Compute σ and σ² with step-by-step explanations.
intro: The Standard Deviation & Variance Calculator measures how spread out a set of numbers is around their mean. **Standard deviation (σ or s)** is the square root of the average squared deviation from the mean, while **variance (σ² or s²)** is that average squared deviation itself. Use this tool whenever you need to quantify data dispersion in statistics, quality control, finance, science experiments, or academic coursework. The distinction between **population** (dividing by N) and **sample** (dividing by N−1, Bessel's correction) is critical and changes your result.
keyTakeaway: **Population σ = √[ Σ(xᵢ − μ)² / N ]**; Sample s = √[ Σ(xᵢ − x̄)² / (N−1) ]. For the classic dataset {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9}: μ = 5, σ = 2, σ² = 4 (population).
useCases: A high school teacher grading a 30-student exam needs to report the class's standard deviation to identify whether scores cluster tightly around the mean or are widely spread.

A quality-control engineer at a manufacturing plant measures the diameter of 50 bolts and uses population standard deviation to determine whether production variance exceeds the ±0.02 mm tolerance spec.

A financial analyst computing the historical volatility of a stock's daily returns over 252 trading days uses sample standard deviation (N−1) because those 252 days are a sample of all possible trading days.

A clinical researcher at a university hospital compares the variance of blood-pressure readings between a treatment group and a control group to assess whether the experimental drug reduces measurement spread.
A meteorologist uses NOAA temperature records to compute the standard deviation of daily high temperatures across a decade, identifying years with abnormally volatile weather patterns.
A student completing AP Statistics homework verifies the step-by-step variance calculation for a small dataset before submitting, using this calculator to check their manual work.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

There are two versions depending on whether your dataset represents an **entire population** or a **sample** drawn from a larger population.

### Population Standard Deviation (σ)

```
μ  = (Σ xᵢ) / N                       ← mean
σ² = Σ(xᵢ − μ)² / N                   ← population variance
σ  = √[ Σ(xᵢ − μ)² / N ]             ← population std dev
```

### Sample Standard Deviation (s)

```
x̄  = (Σ xᵢ) / n                       ← sample mean
s² = Σ(xᵢ − x̄)² / (n − 1)            ← sample variance  (Bessel's correction)
s  = √[ Σ(xᵢ − x̄)² / (n − 1) ]      ← sample std dev
```

> **Why (n−1)?** Bessel's correction compensates for the fact that a sample mean is itself estimated from the data, which slightly underestimates the true population variance. Dividing by (n−1) produces an *unbiased* estimator of σ².

---

## Reference Table

The table below shows standard deviation benchmarks across common real-world domains to give context to your results.

| Domain | Typical Dataset | Mean | Std Dev | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adult male height (US) | NHANES population | 69.1 in | 2.9 in | 68% of men are 66.2–72.0 in tall |
| IQ scores (standardized) | Normative population | 100 | 15 | Score of 130 is +2 σ above mean |
| S&P 500 daily returns | ~252 trading days/yr | ~0.03% | ~1.0% | Annual volatility ≈ 15–20% |
| US adult BMI (CDC NHANES) | National sample | 26.5 | 5.1 | Wide dispersion; not normal dist. |
| Systolic blood pressure | Clinical trials | 120 mmHg | 10–15 mmHg | >2 σ above mean = hypertensive range |
| Classroom exam scores | Typical 30-student class | 75 pts | 10–12 pts | Well-designed test spread |
| Manufacturing bolt diameter | CNC precision part | 10.00 mm | 0.01 mm | 6σ quality: 3.4 defects/million |

---

## Typical Examples with Numbers

### Example 1 — The Classic Textbook Dataset (Population)
Dataset: **2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9** (N = 8)

```
Step 1 — Mean:   μ = (2+4+4+4+5+5+7+9) / 8 = 40 / 8 = 5
Step 2 — Deviations squared:
  (2−5)²=9, (4−5)²=1, (4−5)²=1, (4−5)²=1,
  (5−5)²=0, (5−5)²=0, (7−5)²=4, (9−5)²=16
Step 3 — Sum: 9+1+1+1+0+0+4+16 = 32
Step 4 — Variance:  σ² = 32 / 8 = 4
Step 5 — Std Dev:   σ  = √4 = 2
```
**Result: σ = 2, σ² = 4** — This is the canonical example used in most statistics textbooks.

---

### Example 2 — Sample from a Quality-Control Process
Measured diameters (mm): **9.98, 10.01, 10.00, 9.99, 10.02** (n = 5, sample)

```
Step 1 — Mean:   x̄ = (9.98+10.01+10.00+9.99+10.02) / 5 = 50.00 / 5 = 10.00 mm
Step 2 — Deviations squared:
  (9.98−10.00)²=0.0004, (10.01−10.00)²=0.0001,
  (10.00−10.00)²=0,     (9.99−10.00)²=0.0001,
  (10.02−10.00)²=0.0004
Step 3 — Sum: 0.0010
Step 4 — Sample Variance:  s² = 0.0010 / (5−1) = 0.00025 mm²
Step 5 — Sample Std Dev:   s  = √0.00025 ≈ 0.0158 mm
```
**Result: s ≈ 0.016 mm** — well within a ±0.02 mm tolerance band.

---

### Example 3 — Student Exam Scores (Sample)
Scores: **68, 72, 75, 80, 85, 90, 92** (n = 7)

```
x̄  = (68+72+75+80+85+90+92) / 7 = 562 / 7 ≈ 80.29
Σ(xᵢ − x̄)²  ≈ 149.43 + 68.43 + 28.04 + 0.08 + 22.18 + 94.08 + 134.18 = 496.43
s² = 496.43 / 6 ≈ 82.74
s  ≈ 9.10 points
```
**Result: s ≈ 9.1 points** — a reasonable spread for a 100-point exam.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using population formula on sample data.** Dividing by N instead of (N−1) for a sample *underestimates* the true population standard deviation. For small samples (n < 30), the error is substantial: with n = 5, you underestimate by a factor of √(4/5) ≈ 10.6%.

2. **Confusing standard deviation with standard error (SE).** Standard deviation describes spread within your dataset. Standard error (SE = s / √n) describes uncertainty in your *estimate of the mean*. Mixing them up leads to incorrect confidence intervals.

3. **Forgetting that variance and std dev have different units.** If your data is in meters, variance is in **m²** and standard deviation is in **m**. Reporting variance in the same units as raw data is a dimensional error.

4. **Assuming a small standard deviation means the data is "good."** In some contexts (e.g., biodiversity indices, creative brainstorming scores), high dispersion is desirable. Std dev is a descriptive statistic, not a quality judgment.

5. **Computing std dev on non-numeric or ordinal data.** Standard deviation requires interval or ratio scale data. Calculating it on Likert-scale ordinal responses (e.g., "Strongly Agree = 5") is technically incorrect, though common in practice.

6. **Ignoring outliers' effect on std dev.** Because deviations are *squared*, a single extreme outlier can drastically inflate σ. Always check your dataset for data-entry errors before interpreting results.

---

## Related Calculators

Since no related slugs are defined for this calculator, explore other statistical tools on the site to complement your analysis and verify your results across different methods.

faq.q: What is the difference between population and sample standard deviation?
faq.a: Population standard deviation (σ) divides the sum of squared deviations by N and is used when you have data for every member of the group. Sample standard deviation (s) divides by (N−1) — Bessel's correction — and is used when your data is a subset of a larger population. For large samples (N > 100), the difference is negligible, but for small samples like N = 5, the population formula underestimates true variability by about 10–11%.

faq.q: Why does the classic dataset {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9} give exactly σ = 2?
faq.a: Because the math works out perfectly: the mean is 5, the squared deviations sum to exactly 32, and 32 ÷ 8 = 4 (variance), so √4 = 2. This dataset was deliberately constructed for textbooks to produce clean integers at every step, making it the most widely used teaching example in introductory statistics courses worldwide.

faq.q: What does a standard deviation of 0 mean?
faq.a: A standard deviation of exactly 0 means every value in your dataset is identical — there is zero spread. For example, the dataset {7, 7, 7, 7} has μ = 7 and σ = 0. In practice, σ = 0 can also signal a data-entry error or that a constant was accidentally entered as repeated values.

faq.q: What is the Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 rule) and how does it use standard deviation?
faq.a: For data that follows a normal (bell-curve) distribution, approximately **68%** of values fall within ±1σ of the mean, **95%** within ±2σ, and **99.7%** within ±3σ. For example, with US adult male heights (mean = 69.1 in, σ = 2.9 in), about 95% of men are between 63.3 in and 74.9 in tall. This rule only applies to roughly normal distributions, not skewed or bimodal data.

faq.q: How is standard deviation used in finance and stock analysis?
faq.a: In finance, standard deviation of returns is the primary measure of **volatility**. The S&P 500 has a historical daily return standard deviation of roughly 1.0%, which annualizes to about 15–20% (multiplied by √252 trading days). Portfolio managers use σ to calculate Value-at-Risk (VaR): a portfolio with annualized σ = 15% has approximately a 5% chance of losing more than 24.7% in a given year (under normal distribution assumptions).

faq.q: What is Six Sigma and how does it relate to standard deviation?
faq.a: Six Sigma is a quality-management methodology where the goal is to keep manufacturing defects within **6 standard deviations** of the process mean. Statistically, a ±6σ process (with a 1.5σ mean shift) produces only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. By comparison, a ±3σ process yields 66,807 DPMO. Motorola developed and formalized Six Sigma in the 1980s; it is now widely used in manufacturing, healthcare, and finance.

No. Standard deviation is always **zero or positive** (σ ≥ 0). Because each deviation is squared before summing, the variance is always non-negative, and its square root is therefore also non-negative. A negative value from a manual calculation indicates an arithmetic error — most commonly forgetting to square the individual deviations or using the wrong formula.

faq.q: How many data points do I need for standard deviation to be meaningful?
faq.a: With **n = 2**, you get a valid (but very imprecise) estimate — the standard deviation simply equals half the absolute difference between the two values. Most statisticians consider **n ≥ 30** the threshold at which sample standard deviation becomes a reliably stable estimator of the population σ. For small samples (n < 10), the 95% confidence interval around s is extremely wide, meaning your estimate could be off by 50% or more.

faq.q: What is the coefficient of variation (CV) and how is it related to standard deviation?
faq.a: The Coefficient of Variation (CV) = (σ / μ) × 100%, and it expresses standard deviation as a percentage of the mean. It allows comparison of variability across datasets with different units or scales. For example, if Dataset A has σ = 10, μ = 100 (CV = 10%) and Dataset B has σ = 3, μ = 15 (CV = 20%), Dataset B is actually *more* variable relative to its mean despite having a smaller absolute σ.
